did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluated the diagnostic ability of artificial intelligence chatbots (specifically ChatGPT-4 and Microsoft Copilot) and six online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs) to diagnose melanoma and seborrheic keratosis from images. The study involved inputting images into these AI systems to obtain diagnostic results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is titled 'Testing the ability of artificial intelligence chatbots and dermatologic diagnosis mobile applications to diagnose melanoma and seborrheic keratosis' and describes an original experimental study where the authors tested 6 online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs) and 2 AI chatbots (ChatGPT-4 and Microsoft Copilot) on images of melanoma and seborrheic keratosis to evaluate their diagnostic accuracy. It reports original results from this validation study rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Testing the ability of artificial intelligence chatbots and dermatologic diagnosis mobile applications to diagnose melanoma and seborrheic keratosis
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of two artificial intelligence chatbots (ChatGPT-4 and Microsoft Copilot) and six online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs) for distinguishing melanoma from seborrheic keratosis. The researchers used permissioned images from VisualDx.com, inputting them into the AI systems either directly or with generated patient profiles where required. The evaluation focused on whether the systems correctly identified the lesions as melanoma or seborrheic keratosis, or designated them as 'concerning' versus 'not concerning' for applications providing risk assessments. Each trial was run twice to ensure reliability of the results. The study highlights the growing use of consumer-directed AI for self-diagnosis and aims to inform clinicians about the current capabilities and limitations of these tools.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary diagnostic targets used in the study to evaluate AI chatbots and skin cancer diagnosis applications.
One of the two primary diagnostic targets used in the study; chosen because it can be difficult for lay people to distinguish from melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category (Melanoma vs. Seborrheic Keratosis) or Risk designation (Concerning vs. Not Concerning)
all_task_types.details: The study evaluated the ability of AI chatbots and skin cancer diagnosis applications to correctly diagnose images as melanoma or seborrheic keratosis (binary classification). For applications that did not provide specific diagnoses, the task was judged as correctly designating a lesion as 'concerning' or 'not concerning' (binary classification/risk scoring).

datasets_used.name: VisualDx.com image collection
datasets_used.description: A collection of permissioned images of melanoma and seborrheic keratosis obtained from VisualDx.com for the purpose of testing AI diagnostic tools.

use.testing: Images were input into 6 online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs) and 2 AI chatbots (ChatGPT-4 and Microsoft Copilot) to evaluate their diagnostic accuracy. Each trial was run twice.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive diagnostic accuracy results (e.g., percentages of correct diagnoses) without statistical inference.

VisualDx.com_image_collection.metric: Diagnostic accuracy (correct diagnosis or correct designation as 'concerning'/'not concerning')
VisualDx.com_image_collection.notes: The paper describes the methodology for testing 6 online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs) and 2 AI chatbots (ChatGPT-4 and Microsoft Copilot) on images of melanoma and seborrheic keratosis from VisualDx.com. It states that each trial was run twice. However, the provided text chunks do not contain the specific quantitative results (percentages or counts of correct diagnoses) for any of the models. The text ends before reporting the results section.

architecture_used.main_model: ChatGPT-4, Microsoft Copilot, Dermos, scanoma, Skin-Check, and four other online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs)

architecture_details.model_family: AI chatbots (ChatGPT-4, Microsoft Copilot) and online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs)
architecture_details.input_modality: Images of melanoma and seborrheic keratosis, and optionally generated patient profiles (gender, age range, indoor/outdoor occupation, asymmetry, border, color, size of the lesion)
architecture_details.notes: The study evaluated the diagnostic ability of these systems. Specific internal architectures, layers, heads, or patch sizes for the SCDAs were not reported in the provided text.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Diagnostic category (Melanoma vs. Seborrheic keratosis) or Risk designation ('concerning' vs. 'not concerning')

conclusions: The provided text does not contain the study's main conclusions or results section; it only includes the introduction and methodology.
The paper states that with the proliferation of consumer-directed AI products, many patients are turning to these resources for self-diagnosis.
The authors emphasize the importance for clinicians to be aware of the strengths and limitations of AI chatbots and skin cancer diagnosis applications in diagnosing skin conditions.

The study was designed to test the ability of 6 online skin cancer diagnosis applications (SCDAs) and 2 AI chatbots (ChatGPT-4 and Microsoft Copilot) to correctly diagnose images of melanoma and seborrheic keratosis.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its retrospective nature, recall bias, and selection bias, as well as restriction to a single tertiary care center (Sainte-Justine University Hospital Center).
The dataset consists of permissioned images from VisualDx.com, which may introduce dataset bias regarding the specific population or image characteristics of that source.
The study design is a diagnostic accuracy test rather than a clinical trial, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings where patient history and physical examination are available.
The paper does not report specific quantitative results (accuracy percentages) in the provided text chunks, only the methodology and limitations.

The evaluation included both specific diagnoses (melanoma vs. seborrheic keratosis) and risk designations ('concerning' vs. 'not concerning'), which may have different clinical implications and error rates.
